text: Four 8mm robotic trocars are placed across the upper abdomen. Laparoscopic scissors are used to open the splenic cyst, and approximately 850 cc's of brown fluid are aspirated. The robotic system is then docked.
The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions, addressing dense adhesions between the abdominal wall, liver, and omentum using robotic instruments.

Endocyanine green dye is administered to visually delineate the interface between the normal splenic parenchyma and the cyst capsule. The surgeon marks the margin with a monopolar hook and uses a vessel sealer to divide the anterior cyst wall from the splenic parenchyma.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) dye to identify the border between the splenic capsule and spleen at the superior medial aspect. The final attachments are divided using a vessel sealer, completing the separation of the cyst from the splenic parenchyma.
The surgeon irrigates the cyst cavity and evaluates it for hemostasis. A bleeding vessel is identified and treated using a bipolar device. The cavity is irrigated again to ensure hemostasis.

An omental patch is positioned to fill the cyst cavity, and a 19 French Blake drain is placed. One of the 8mm trocars is removed, and the site is enlarged to facilitate the placement of a laparoscopic retrieval bag, which is used to remove the specimen.
